data.title: CDC changes stance on vaccine link to autism claims
data.description:
The CDC altered a webpage Wednesday dealing with autism and vaccines to state that the idea that vaccines don't cause autism "is not an evidence-based claim."

The page notes that "studies have not ruled out the possibility that infant vaccines cause autism," adding that "Studies supporting a link have been ignored by health authorities."

The development was first reported by The New York Times.

The site now contains information often cited by HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., including that the rise in autism diagnoses since the 1980s coincides with more childhood vaccines administered.

"Though the cause of autism is likely to be multi-factorial, the scientific foundation to rule out one potential contributor entirely has not been established," the CDC now says.

The page still contains a header reading "Vaccines do not cause Autism." A note at the bottom states that the header is remaining following an agreement with Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.), a medical doctor who has sparred with RFK Jr. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Maximus projects $8.10 EPS and $475M free cash flow for fiscal 2026 while advancing AI and federal growth initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Maximus (MMS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Bruce L. Caswell, President and CEO, highlighted that "revenue and profitability came in higher than projected, reflecting both the strength of our core operations and the disciplined execution of our strategy throughout the year." Caswell emphasized a 3.9% organic growth rate for the consolidated business, with U.S. Federal Services delivering 12.1% organic growth and outside the U.S. segment delivering 4.1% organic growth.
  • Caswell stated, "contractual relationships remained stable and secure throughout the year, with cancellations or impacts at just 0.5% of fiscal year 2025 revenue, a figure that is unchanged from our prior earnings call." He further noted the company’s confidence in future growth, driven by investments in AI and technology-enabled government solutions.
  • Strategic priorities for fiscal 2026 include expanding in U.S. federal markets, capitalizing on opportunities from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, and deploying AI and automation across operations. Caswell shared details on a new $86 million Air Force contract and the company’s first cooperative research and development agreement (CRADA) to bolster intellectual property and national security capabilities.
  • "Our Total Experience Management or TXM solution, which I've mentioned on prior calls, is a FedRAMP secure, modular, flexible, scalable and configurable platform that helps enable federal agencies to deliver smarter citizen-centric services," said Caswell. He also highlighted 30 AI-related deployments planned or in progress, varying from pilots to full-scale implementations.
  • Caswell indicated that signed awards for fiscal 2025 totaled $4.7 billion, with a book-to-bill of approximately 0.9x for the trailing 12 months and a pipeline at September 30 of $51.3 billion.
  • David Mutryn, CFO, stated, "revenue totaled $5.43 billion. This equates to organic growth of 3.9% over the prior year. From an earnings standpoint, the full year adjusted EBITDA margin was 12.9% and adjusted earnings per share were $7.36." Mutryn also reported, "free cash flow for the full fiscal year 2025" was $366 million and highlighted a focus on debt paydown and share repurchases totaling $457 million.

Outlook

  • Fiscal 2026 revenue is projected to be between $5.225 billion and $5.425 billion, with a midpoint of $5.325 billion. Adjusted EPS is projected to be between $7.95 and $8.25, with a midpoint of $8.10 per share. Free cash flow is guided to a midpoint of $475 million, representing about 30% year-over-year growth.
  • Mutryn explained, "The midpoint of $5.325 billion of revenue reflects our current view of volume dynamics on some of our variable work." He added, "the $8.10 midpoint of adjusted EPS guidance reflects ongoing margin expansion and 10% growth over fiscal 2025."
  • Management anticipates that some excess volumes in fiscal 2025 will not recur in fiscal 2026, resulting in a year-over-year revenue headwind of approximately 3%, partially offset by 1% organic growth. Margin guidance has improved to 13.7% for adjusted EBITDA.

Financial Results

  • For fiscal year 2025, revenue increased 2.4% to $5.43 billion. The U.S. Federal Services segment registered $3.07 billion in revenue, with a 15.3% operating income margin. The U.S. Services segment posted $1.76 billion in revenue and a 9.7% operating margin, impacted by $16 million in severance costs.
  • Outside the U.S., revenue fell to $600 million, primarily due to prior divestitures, but organic growth reached 4.1% and the segment margin improved to 3.7%. Cash flows from operating activities totaled $429 million, and fourth quarter free cash flow was $642 million. Net leverage stood at 1.5x at year-end.
  • Mutryn noted, "severance charges totaling approximately $16 million...had a more pronounced effect on the operating margins of the U.S. Services segment." He expects these cost management efforts to support margin expansion in fiscal 2026.

Q&A

  • Will, CJS Securities: Asked about the drivers behind the strong EBITDA margin guidance for 2026. Mutryn responded, "all 3 (segments) are actually slightly higher than where they finished fiscal year '25...the continued deployment of technology and automation as well as cost management."
  • Will, CJS Securities: Inquired about segment level revenue growth. Mutryn explained that both U.S. Federal and U.S. Services may see mild contraction, with more erosion on the federal side due to overperformance in 2025. However, the federal segment also has the strongest near-term pipeline.
  • Will, CJS Securities: Asked about the impact of the government shutdown. Caswell answered, "We really don't anticipate any negative impacts on our delivery on our contract portfolio in Q1 FY '26. Nearly all of our programs were deemed essential services by the government."
  • Will, CJS Securities: Sought details on capital allocation and M&A priorities. Caswell said the focus remains on disciplined M&A in the U.S. federal market, particularly defense and national security, with priorities on customer relationships, technical capabilities, and business systems.
  • Will, CJS Securities: Requested an update on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Caswell stated, "there's been no real update from a policy perspective and not surprisingly, no regulatory updates either...the SNAP payment error rate issue...can lead to a significant financial lift for states who don't bring their error rates down below 6%." He described ongoing engagement with states, demonstrations, and technology solutions to address error rates.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and constructive tone, seeking clarity on margin drivers, segment trends, capital allocation, and policy-related opportunities. No adversarial exchanges or pressing skepticism were evident.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Caswell stating, "We are proud of what we accomplished, and we are energized by the momentum we're carrying into fiscal 2026." During Q&A, management remained detailed and optimistic, emphasizing their strategic advantages and readiness for future opportunities.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, sentiment remained positive, with management continuing to express confidence in growth initiatives and analysts focusing on execution details and future drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is now formalized with revenue midpoint at $5.325 billion and adjusted EPS midpoint at $8.10, compared to early color in Q3, which expected flat or slightly lower revenue and improved margins.
  • Margin guidance has increased, with adjusted EBITDA margin projected at 13.7%, above the high end of the previous target range. Management cited ongoing technology and cost initiatives.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from clarifying the timing and impact of policy-driven growth in Q3 to seeking more granular details on margin improvement and segment performance in Q4.
  • Management's tone has been consistently confident, now reinforced by specific contract wins, AI deployment progress, and a larger pipeline. Analysts’ sentiment remained positive but increasingly sought specificity on execution and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted that some revenue headwinds are anticipated in fiscal 2026 due to the non-recurrence of excess volumes and unpredictable natural disaster support.
  • Caswell noted the possibility of delayed procurement activity and the need for states to address SNAP payment error rates to avoid federal funding reductions.
  • Delays in customer payments and the risk of future government shutdowns were acknowledged, but management indicated minimal anticipated impacts due to the essential nature of Maximus’ services and diversified contract base.

Final Takeaway

Maximus enters fiscal 2026 with formal guidance targeting $5.325 billion in revenue, $8.10 adjusted EPS, and $475 million in free cash flow, underpinned by strong customer relationships, disciplined cost management, and strategic investments in AI and federal markets. Management underscores the durability of its contract portfolio and sees significant future opportunity from policy-driven initiatives and technology adoption, particularly positioning the company for potential accelerated growth in fiscal 2027 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cleveland Fed Pres. Hammack sees inflation as ongoing concern despite labor market softening
data.description:
Cleveland Federal Reserve Bank President Beth Hammack believes the Fed must maintain a somewhat restrictive monetary policy stance despite signs of softening in the labor market, as inflation pressures remain significant.

In an interview with CNBC, Hammack emphasized that recent economic data presents a challenging balancing act for monetary policymakers trying to fulfill their dual mandate.

“We are seeing some signs of softening in the labor market, but we’re still seeing elevated signs of inflation,” Hammack said.

She noted that while the recent jobs report showed mixed results, the Cleveland Fed is also monitoring other indicators such as ADP jobs data and layoff notices, which have been “moving up slightly.”

Hammack highlighted the persistent impact of inflation on households, particularly among workers in her district. “The pressure from inflation is still really significant and that they’re using their paychecks just for food and housing and energy,” she said, adding that consumers “really don’t have anything left over at the end of the month.”

Regarding monetary policy, Hammack believes current rates are “barely restrictive, if at all” and stressed that “we need to maintain a somewhat restrictive stance to bring inflation back down to our 2% objective.”

She noted that some inflation indicators, particularly in core services excluding housing, have been “trending upwards not downwards” over recent months.

The Cleveland Fed president also addressed financial stability concerns, particularly in private credit markets and emerging technologies like AI.

While acknowledging positive aspects of private credit, including “reasonably stable funding” with “seven-to-ten-year lockups,” she expressed concern about connections to the banking system through “significant growth in lending” from banks to private credit entities.

When asked about interest rates, Hammack indicated that current rates seem “right around a neutral rate” and “fairly restrictive.”

Drawing from her market experience in the 1990s, she remarked that the current yield curve around 4% represents “actually pretty modest interest rates” compared to historical standards.

Earlier on Thursday, Hammack prepared a speech for the Cleveland Fed's 2025 Financial Stability Conference, where she warned that easing monetary policy too quickly or too much could also endanger financial stability.

More on the US economy

  • Monetary policy, private credit, stablecoins can threaten financial stability, Fed's Hammack says
  • Treasury Market Scuttles Hope For December Rate Cut, Doubts January Cut, After Hawkish Fed Minutes
  • Fed minutes show divide in views on direction of policy at October meeting
  • Delinquencies, charge-offs rise as consumer sentiment weakens: October Credit Pulse

data.title: Warner Music Group outlines 150–200 basis points margin improvement in 2026 as AI and market share gains accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Warner Music Group Corp. (WMG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Kyncl, President, CEO & Director, highlighted, "we've improved our market share and delivered profitable growth, all while realigning our company to capitalize on the tremendous set of opportunities we have ahead." He reported that in the U.S., market share increased by 0.6 percentage points year-over-year and globally, WMG's share of the Spotify top 200 rose by 6 percentage points versus fiscal 2024. Kyncl also cited new deals with key DSP partners, stating, "our new agreements with key DSP partners better reflect music's ever-growing value and provide greater certainty around our economics."

  • Kyncl referenced the company's proactive approach to generative AI, explaining, "we've developed a set of principles that will govern how we engage with AI platforms. We will only make agreements with partners who commit to licensed models while securing economic terms that properly reflect the value of music."

  • Armin Zerza, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Q4 has been a quarter of acceleration as we delivered record high quarterly revenue as well as our highest year-over-year growth in nearly 2 years." Zerza highlighted a 13% total revenue growth and 64% growth in artist services, crediting "WMX led merch campaigns for Oasis and My Chemical Romance." He added, "we are on track to deliver against our reorganization and related cost savings program of $200 million in annualized savings in 2026, increasing to $300 million in 2027."

Outlook

  • The company expects to see strong top line growth in 2026, driven by organic investments, high-impact accretive M&A, and contributions from distribution and direct-to-consumer offerings. Zerza noted, "we expect to see strong top line growth, which we look to bolster through focused organic investments and initiatives in our core music business and high-impact accretive M&A as well as contribution from adjacent areas such as distribution and direct-to-consumer offerings."

  • WMG is targeting adjusted OIBDA margin improvement of 150 to 200 basis points in 2026, with cost savings increasing sequentially throughout the year. Zerza said, "we are on track to deliver against our reorganization and related cost savings program of $200 million in annualized savings in 2026, increasing to $300 million in 2027."

  • The company sees "tremendous potential in new incremental growth areas, particularly in AI licensing deals, which we plan to discuss in future calls."

Financial Results

  • Total revenue grew 13% in Q4 2025, with double-digit growth in Recorded Music and Music Publishing.

  • Recorded Music subscription streaming increased 8.4% and ad-supported streaming grew 3% on an adjusted basis.

  • Artist services revenue rose 64%, attributed to merch campaigns for Oasis and My Chemical Romance.

  • Adjusted OIBDA rose by 12%, but margins declined slightly due to a revenue mix shift towards lower-margin artist service revenue.

  • As of September 30, cash balance was $532 million, total debt $4.4 billion, and net debt $3.8 billion.

Q&A

  • Kutgun Maral, Evercore ISI: Asked about WMG's role in capturing incremental value from rights monetization amid AI and DSP innovation. Kyncl responded, "we are determined and have decided that we're the drivers, not the passengers of this incremental opportunity" and outlined WMG's principles on AI dealmaking: "We'll do agreements with partners who commit to licensed models. We'll do it on economic terms that properly reflect the value of music ... artists and songwriters have the opportunity and right to opt in for any new songs that implicate their name, image, likeness and voice."

  • Benjamin Black, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about building blocks for 2026 top line growth and margin expansion. Zerza stated, "we will, of course, benefit from the contractual wholesale price increases that we have agreed now with several top DSPs," and reiterated confidence in margin expansion, targeting "mid- to high 20s" margins in the long term.

  • Peter Supino, Wolfe Research: Asked about market share gains and label performance. Kyncl replied, "Our market share hasn't grown just in 1 or 2 places. It's really been broad-based across both our flagship labels as well as all of our regions."

  • Michael Morris, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about M&A and distribution growth. Zerza explained, "we have a very strong pipeline in place, which, as I mentioned, we expect to start to materialize starting in calendar year '26," and highlighted new leadership in distribution as a catalyst for growth.

  • Ian Moore, Bernstein: Questioned the commercial opportunity in AI licensing. Kyncl described the AI environment as "a very energizing moment in the industry" and emphasized WMG's strategy to "get in early, set the terms and define the future for us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on monetization opportunities, margin expansion, and the sustainability of market share gains, expressing a neutral to slightly positive tone, with questions seeking clarity on execution and upside but no overt skepticism.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we are determined" and "we are confident," while emphasizing proactive strategy and broad-based success.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with less focus on organizational tension and more discussion of tangible results and future initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 showed a clear acceleration in revenue and market share versus Q3, with revenue growth rising to 13% from 7% in the prior quarter.

  • Management's language shifted from describing strategic plans and organizational changes in Q3 to celebrating broad-based execution and market share gains in Q4.

  • Analysts in Q4 probed more about execution details and future growth levers, especially in AI and M&A, compared to Q3’s focus on restructuring and cost initiatives.

  • Margin expansion guidance remained consistent, but Q4 featured more specifics on savings timelines and new revenue streams.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company acknowledged the potential risks of generative AI, emphasizing the need to "protect the rights of our artists and songwriters while simultaneously growing new revenue streams on their behalf."

  • Management highlighted ongoing cost control and efficiency measures as crucial for sustaining margin improvement.

  • Analysts raised concerns about maintaining market share gains and the balance between cost savings and reinvestment for growth.

Final Takeaway

Warner Music Group closed fiscal 2025 with accelerating revenue growth, improved market share, and a commitment to driving further top and bottom line gains in 2026, underpinned by new DSP deals, proactive engagement with AI platforms, and an ongoing cost savings program targeting $200 million in annualized savings. Management projects strong momentum into 2026, supported by operational streamlining, strategic investments, and a robust pipeline for M&A and new business models.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dividend heroes: Brown-Forman among the impressive list of 40+ year dividend raisers
data.description:
Brown-Forman (BF.B) (BF.A) rose 3.0% in Thursday afternoon trading and is now back above its 50-day moving average. Notably, the spirits company announced on Wednesday that it was increasing its dividend for the 42nd consecutive year.

The new quarterly cash dividend will go to $.2310 per share, up 2% from the prior $0.2265 per share payout rate. The dividend is payable on January 2, 2026, to stockholders of record on December 5, 2025. The dividend yield for new buyers of the stock is 3.35%.

Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B) highlighted that it has now boosted its dividend rate for 42 straight years in what it says is a testament to the strength of its balance sheet, the confidence management has in the ability to generate strong cash flow, and the commitment to returning cash to shareholders.

While the dividend streak is impressive, there are some notable consumer companies with even longer streaks of annual dividend increases. That list of dividend heroes includes Genuine Parts (GPC) and Procter & Gamble (PG) at 69 years, Coca-Cola (KO) at 63 years, Hormel Foods (HRL) at 59 years, Altria (MO) at 56 years, and Target (TGT) at 54 years.

data.title: Market Voices: Waymo adds more cities, Fed's Hammack on rates
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo said that it plans to begin manually driven robotaxi service in Minneapolis, Tampa, and New Orleans, with driverless service beginning as soon as next year.

The company intends to wait until its technology is validated in those cities before committing to service launches in those areas, Waymo spokesperson Ethan Teicher told CNBC via email. 

“2026 is very much on the table, but we’ll be led by our safety framework,” Ethan Teicher added.

Earlier this week, Waymo announced it had begun limited driverless service in Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, Miami, and Orlando, with plans to offer the service to the general public beginning next year. 

Waymo already operates in Austin, the San Francisco Bay Area, Phoenix, Atlanta, and Los Angeles. The company previously announced plans to expand into Detroit, Denver, Las Vegas, London, Nashville, San Diego, and Washington, D.C., next year, CNBC noted.

Other companies involved in developing robotaxi services include Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox, Tesla (TSLA), Baidu (BIDU), Pony AI (PONY), Uber (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT). 

  • Cleveland Federal Reserve President Beth Hammack told CNBC on Thursday that she thinks interest rates need to be somewhat restrictive in order to bring down the inflation rate. 

“I think that we need to maintain a modestly, somewhat restrictive stance of policy to make sure that we are continuing to bring inflation back down to our 2% objective,” Hammock said on CNBC's Squawk on the Street.

“Right now, to me, monetary policy is barely restrictive, if at all, and I think we need to make sure that we’re maintaining that somewhat restrictive stance to bring monetary to bring in place," she added.

Hammock is currently an alternate voting member of the FOMC.

data.title: Paramount wins broadcast rights for Champions League games in UK from 2027-31: report
data.description:
Paramount Skydance (PSKY) has won the auction for the rights to broadcast most Champions League matches in the UK from 2027 to 2031, according to a report by The Guardian on Thursday.

The report said Paramount made the largest bid for the league's rights at the auction.

Sources told the news outlet that Paramount’s bid was considerably higher than the £1B currently paid by TNT in what will be regarded as a successful auction for the UEFA-owned joint venture UC3, which runs the Champions League, and its new commercial rights distributor, Relevent Football Partners.

Paramount’s deal would give them every Champions League game live in the UK other than the first pick of Tuesday night matches, the rights to which were won by Amazon Prime (AMZN), the report said.

data.title: Ivanhoe Mines opens long-awaited South African platinum mine
data.description:
Ivanhoe Mines (OTCQX:IVPAF) has formally inaugurated its long-awaited Platreef mine in South Africa, which could become one of the world's biggest platinum producers if plans to eventually supply more than 1 million oz/year are realized, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

Platreef is planned to ramp up production to 100,000 oz/year of platinum, palladium, rhodium and gold, before targeting 450,000 oz/year in its second phase from ~2028.

Platreef's history shows how it can take decades to transform promising geology into operating mines: The project was first conceived in the 1980s, before Ivanhoe’s (OTCQX:IVPAF) subsidiary received a prospecting permit in 2000 and converted that to mining rights in 2014.

The new mine begins production as platinum and palladium have rallied this year, rising as much as 71% and 54%, respectively, following a protracted market slump.

data.title: September jobs report puts the Fed in a 'will they, won't they, and should they?' spot
data.description:
For watchers of monetary policy and the Federal Reserve, the release of the much-delayed September jobs report on Thursday presented a largely mixed picture in terms of hawkishness and dovishness, leaving the central bank in a spot that Wells Fargo described as a "will they, won't they, and should they?" situation.

The Fed cut interest rates by 50 basis points across September and October, citing downside risks to the labor market. But the huge headline number beat in Thursday's report indicated that job growth remains strong.

On the other hand, diving deeper into the data, both July and August nonfarm payrolls saw significant revisions. Moreover, September's unemployment rate of 4.4% is the highest since October 2021. This suggests that there are underlying cracks in the labor market. 

"The shutdown-delayed September jobs report did not provide clarity on what the FOMC will do at its much-debated December meeting," Wells Fargo's Sarah House said. 

"The unemployment rate climbed to 4.44%, just shy of rounding up to 4.5%. A tick higher in the labor force participation rate suggests some of this unemployment rate increase was driven by better supply growth, but nonetheless it was the highest unemployment rate reading since October 2021 and points to the FOMC struggling to maintain the 'maximum employment' part of its mandate," House added.

Complicating matters further, the Fed will not receive any more nonfarm payroll reports ahead of its final monetary policy committee meeting of the year in December. Fed speakers during the U.S. government shutdown had already cautioned against further interest rate cuts amid the paucity of economic data. 

"The Federal Reserve is still driving in the fog ... The downward revisions for the last two months leave us in an environment where we are not creating enough payroll jobs to keep unemployment from rising. As there will not be an October jobs report, and the November report will not be released until after the Fed meets in December, the odds on a rate cut have diminished significantly," Art Hogan, chief market strategist at B. Riley Wealth, said. 

According to the CME FedWatch tool, the odds of another 25 basis point interest rate cut in December currently stand at 41.4%, versus 30.1% a day ago and 50.1% a week ago. 

The blowout September jobs report contributed to a massive open for Wall Street earlier in the day. Sentiment has since soured, leading to a wipeout of all morning gains. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

data.title: Insulet drops on investor day as long-term outlook unveiled
data.description:
Shares of Insulet Corporation (PODD) slipped ~7% on Thursday as the insulin pump maker announced its long-term financial outlook during its 2025 Investor Day at the company’s headquarters in Acton, Massachusetts.

At the event, the company announced plans to expand its topline at a compound annual growth rate of roughly 20% at constant currency rates for 2025–2028, thanks to improved adoption of its Omnipod automated insulin delivery system and global expansion.

Additionally, Insulet (PODD) projects its adjusted diluted earnings per share to grow at more than 25% CAGR during the period, backed by an estimated 100 bps yearly expansion of its adjusted operating margin amid operational efficiencies and increased scale.

In terms of pipeline, the company said it plans to introduce enhancements to its Omnipod 5 platform next year, subject to FDA clearance, and looks to achieve full CGM integration with all major glucose sensors. Insulet’s (PODD) next CGM device, Omnipod 6, is expected to launch in 2027, targeting patients with type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
